antipattern.symptom: Migration drops integer id columns and recreates as UUID. Every ForeignKey on the table breaks. Squashed migrations no longer apply. Backup restore fails.
antipattern.root-cause: Developer believed "switching to UUID is just changing the field type". The PK swap requires renaming the column, rewiring every FK in the schema, and rewriting every cached query plan.
antipattern.fix: Reject the PK swap. Apply rule r1-separate-uid: keep integer id as PK; add a separate `uid` UUIDField for external IDs; expose `uid` in API responses; internal joins continue to use id.

antipattern.symptom: Restoring a soft-deleted row fails with IntegrityError: duplicate key violates unique constraint. The deleted row still occupies the unique index.
antipattern.root-cause: Field declared as `email = models.EmailField(unique=True)` on a soft-deletable model. Partial-index requirement was missed in code review.
antipattern.fix: Replace `unique=True` with a `Meta.constraints = [UniqueConstraint(fields=['email'], condition=Q(deleted_at__isnull=True), name='unique_active_email')]`. The validator rule r3-partial-unique-under-soft-delete catches this.

antipattern.symptom: Django admin shows zero deleted users even when querying by deleted_at. loaddata fails to overwrite soft-deleted rows. ORM-level signals fire on hard delete only.
antipattern.root-cause: Developer set `objects = SoftDeleteManager()` and removed the default Manager. Tools that expect Model.objects to return ALL rows (admin, loaddata, some migration ops) silently lose access.
antipattern.fix: Preserve both managers: `objects = SoftDeleteManager()` (default — excludes deleted) AND `all_objects = models.Manager()` (every row). Configure admin's `get_queryset` to use all_objects with a "Include deleted" filter.

antipattern.symptom: Soft-deleting a Customer hard-deletes their Orders. Compliance review fails: "deleted" customer's order history is gone.
antipattern.root-cause: Order.customer = ForeignKey(Customer, on_delete=models.CASCADE). Customer.delete() (soft) triggers QuerySet.delete() on children via cascade — which is the ORM's hard-delete path.
antipattern.fix: Pick on_delete=PROTECT (cannot soft-delete a Customer with active Orders) or SET_NULL (orphan the Order, retain history). The spec's foreign_keys[].on_delete and reason fields force the explicit choice.
